pattern: Summed across both halves, a distinct prime is counted twice exactly when it straddles the cut, so the best split is the point covered by the most prime occurrence-spans.
hints: For any fixed split, the combined distinct-prime count equals the number of distinct primes present plus the number of those primes that appear on both sides.
A prime appears on both sides of cut k precisely when k lies strictly after its first occurrence and at or before its last occurrence.

Treat each prime's first-to-last occurrence as an interval of valid cut positions; the answer is the count of distinct primes plus the maximum number of such intervals overlapping at a single cut, recomputed as updates change the array.

edge_inputs.description: same prime on both sides straddles
update creates a straddling duplicate prime
one is not prime, boundary value

stress.description: small arrays and small values with frequent prime repeats and persistent updates
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 40))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 20) for _ in range(n)]
    q = rng.randint(1, 30)
    queries = []
    for _ in range(q):
        idx = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        val = rng.randint(1, 20)
        queries.append([idx, val])
    return [nums, queries]
